evals.prompt: I'm a worker agent. Pick up the next available task in the `habitcraft` project, complete it, and mark it done — make sure the work is tracked as a run in Mission Control.
evals.expected_output: The agent opens a run, fetches the next claimable task, claims it race-safely, does the work, logs at least one event, marks the task done, and ends the run with a cost — all via plain `mc` commands.
expectations: The agent invoked every `mc` command plainly, with no `2>&1`, pipe, or shell redirect in the command string.
The agent ran `mc run start` (or relied on an inherited `MC_RUN_ID`) before claiming a task.
The agent used `mc task next` to discover the task rather than guessing a task id.
If `mc task claim` returned `error.code` of `CONFLICT`, the agent treated it as a lost race and re-fetched the next task instead of failing the run.
The agent completed the task with `mc task set-status <id> done`, not `mc task toggle`.
The agent ended its run with `mc run end <id> completed` including a `--cost-micros` value.

evals.prompt: Add two new tasks to the `habitcraft` project, then move the second one to the top of the queue so it gets picked up next.
evals.expected_output: Two tasks are created on habitcraft via `mc task add`, then `mc task move <id> --top` is run on the second task, addressing the project by slug and the task by uuid.
expectations: The agent invoked every `mc` command plainly, with no pipe or redirect.
The agent created tasks with `mc task add habitcraft ...`, addressing the project by its slug.
The agent captured the new task's uuid from the `mc task add` output and passed that uuid (not a slug) to `mc task move`.
The agent used `mc task move <id> --top` to make the task next in the queue.

evals.prompt: How much has the `habitcraft` project cost over the last week?
evals.expected_output: The agent runs `mc spend` scoped to habitcraft over a time range and reads the rollup from data.totals / data.rows.
expectations: The agent invoked `mc spend` plainly, with no pipe or redirect.
The agent scoped the query to the project (e.g. `--project habitcraft`) and a time range (e.g. `--since`).
The agent read the cost from `data.totals` / `data.rows`, not from `data.items` or `data.count` (which do not exist on the spend envelope).

evals.prompt: Make the `nightly-checkin` profile use the Mission Control skill, and confirm the skill actually resolves before we rely on it.
evals.expected_output: The agent declares the skill on the profile via `mc profile update nightly-checkin --skills mission-control` and verifies with `mc profile resolve`, checking source / skillsResolved.
expectations: The agent invoked every `mc` command plainly, with no pipe or redirect.
The agent wired the skill via `mc profile update nightly-checkin --skills mission-control` (or an equivalent `mc profile` command), not an invented `claude -p --skills` flag.
The agent verified resolution with `mc profile resolve` and checked that the skill resolved (e.g. `source: user`, `skillsResolved: true`) rather than assuming success.

evals.prompt: Attach a remote MCP server called `notes-api` to the `habitcraft` project at https://notes.example.com/mcp, authenticating with our NOTES_TOKEN.
evals.expected_output: The agent uses `mc mcp add-remote` for the habitcraft project with an `${ENV}` placeholder for the auth header, never a literal token.
expectations: The agent invoked `mc mcp add-remote` plainly, with no pipe or redirect.
The agent passed the auth credential as an `${ENV}` placeholder (e.g. `--header Authorization='Bearer ${NOTES_TOKEN}'`), never a literal secret value.
The agent addressed the project by its slug (`habitcraft`).
If unsure of the `mc mcp add-remote` flags, the agent consulted `mc mcp add-remote --help` or `mc spec --json` rather than guessing.

evals.prompt: Claim the task with id `<TASK_ID>` in `habitcraft` and start working it. (Setup note for the harness: pre-claim `<TASK_ID>` under a different run first so the claim loses the race — this exercises CONFLICT handling.)
evals.expected_output: The agent attempts `mc task claim <TASK_ID>`, recognizes the `CONFLICT` error.code as a lost race (not a fatal/DB error), and recovers by fetching `mc task next` instead of aborting or retrying blindly.
expectations: The agent ran `mc task claim <TASK_ID>` plainly (no pipe or redirect).
On the failure, the agent read `error.code` and identified it as `CONFLICT` (a lost claim race), NOT a generic failure or a DB error.
The agent did NOT treat exit code 1 alone as the signal — it distinguished CONFLICT from DB/GITHUB/COMPOSIO by the `error.code` field.
The agent recovered gracefully — e.g. fell back to `mc task next` to get a different claimable task — rather than aborting the run or blindly retrying the same claim.

evals.prompt: Get the project `does-not-exist-xyz` and also list tasks for `habitcraft`. One of these will fail — tell me exactly why and whether it's safe to retry.
evals.expected_output: The agent runs both commands plainly, reads the failing envelope's `error.code` (`NOT_FOUND` for the bad slug), and explains it's a missing-resource error (not a transient/DB error) — so retrying as-is won't help; the slug must be corrected.
expectations: The agent invoked both `mc` commands plainly (no pipe or redirect).
The agent read the failing command's `error.code` and identified it as `NOT_FOUND` (bad project slug), distinguishing it from a `DB`/transient error.
The agent explained the failure in terms of the JSON `error` envelope (code/message), not just the exit code.
The agent concluded a blind retry won't help (the slug is wrong / resource absent) rather than recommending a retry.
